fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out our error reporting client library. The core idea is that devs drop this into their app and it handles all the messy stuff — capturing errors, cleaning up sensitive data before it goes out, keeping a running log of what happened leading up to a crash, and shipping the report off to the right place depending on whether we're talking browser or server context.

One thing that burned us before (remember that login module incident?) is we kept leaking things like passwords and tokens in query strings and form data. So redaction needs to be really solid — both in URLs and in nested object data. Same substring-match approach we've used elsewhere.

We also had issues with deeply recursive objects crashing the serializer, so there needs to be some kind of depth-limiting safety net.

For the browser side, we need to hook into the global error surfaces and make sure cross-origin noise doesn't spam us. The server side needs clean HTTP delivery with proper callbacks.

Also — and this is the one people keep forgetting — we shouldn't be firing off reports in local dev environments unless someone has explicitly said they want that. We've had too many dev machines polluting production dashboards.

Middleware integration should clear per-request state cleanly and forward errors down the chain. The breadcrumb trail should cap out at a reasonable size so memory doesn't balloon. Can someone own this end to end?

A couple extra specifics from the questions that came in: the filtering should be case-insensitive and substring-based everywhere we use it, both for filterUrl and filterData. So if a configured term shows up anywhere inside a key name, that value gets replaced with the literal string '[FILTERED]'. That means a filter of 'secret' should catch 'secret_key', 'my_secret', and 'SECRET', and this is the same behavior we want because of the earlier leak where things like 'password', 'secret', and auth tokens were getting sent in plain text in URL query params and structured POST body data. The shared matching behavior lives in src/core/filter.ts (or equivalent), but the important product behavior is just that any key whose name contains a filter term gets redacted to '[FILTERED]'.

On the sanitizer side, if nobody passes maxDepth, the default cap is 8. Anything deeper than that becomes '[DEPTH]'. Recursive references and non-serializable values should turn into stable neutral marker strings, and absent or undefined object fields should just be left out of the output entirely. For stack traces, we’re parsing line by line and only accepting the usual V8/Node.js shapes: 'at <method> (<file>:<line>:<column>)' or 'at <file>:<line>:<column>'. Each parsed frame needs to come out as exactly four fields: 'file', 'method', 'number', and 'column'. If a line doesn’t fit, skip it, and if nothing matches at all, return an empty array.

For browser handling, one specific drop rule we do want is to silently ignore events where message === 'Script error' and line === 0, since that’s the cross-origin browser noise we talked about. For DOM descriptions, use a name fragment made from the tag plus an optional nth-child qualifier when there are siblings of the same type, include id/class in that fragment when present, and build a CSS selector path rooted at body by walking down through ancestors. Text content can be included, but if it runs long, truncate to 300 characters and append '...'. Other than id and class, attributes should stay limited to the permitted set.

Also confirming delivery rules because there was some confusion there: we only send when an API key is configured, reporting is not explicitly disabled, and the environment is not 'development' or 'test'. The one override is if 'reportData' is explicitly set to true in config, in which case we still send even in development or test. Missing API key always blocks delivery no matter what. And for breadcrumbs, keep only the newest 40 records, dropping the oldest when a new one comes in after the trail is full. If breadcrumbs are enabled, sending a notice should automatically add one with message 'Honeybadger Notice', and that one counts toward the same 40-record cap. If no category is provided, default it to 'custom'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: notify(notice: string | Error | NoticeData, config?: ClientConfig, context?: ContextData): boolean

trigger_keywords: how are headers mapped in cgi_data
what prefix do headers get in cgi data
how does header transformation work for reports
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When notice headers are included in the payload, they are merged into cgi_data with their keys uppercased and prefixed with 'HTTP_' (e.g., a header key 'other' becomes 'HTTP_OTHER'). The original header keys without prefix are also retained in cgi_data alongside the prefixed versions. Filter rules apply to both the prefixed and un-prefixed versions.

trigger_keywords: how should cross-origin script errors be handled
what happens with Script error in global error handler
should we ignore browser cross-origin errors
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Global error events where the message is exactly 'Script error' (case-sensitive) AND the line number is 0 are treated as cross-origin script errors and silently ignored — no notice is created and no breadcrumb is recorded. Any other error message, even with line 0, proceeds normally. This prevents noise from third-party scripts that the page does not have CORS access to.

trigger_keywords: how are tags deduplicated and ordered
what is the precedence order for tags
how do notice context and config tags combine
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tags are collected in precedence order: notice tags first, then context tags, then configured (global) tags. After collection, all tag strings are split on commas, each segment is stripped of any non-word characters (only \w characters retained), blank entries are removed, and finally duplicates are eliminated while preserving the first-seen order. The result is a clean ordered array with no duplicates.

trigger_keywords: what HTTP endpoint does browser delivery use
where does the browser client post notices
what is the JS notice submission URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Browser delivery sends a POST request to 'https://api.honeybadger.io/v1/notices/js' with the request made asynchronously. Required headers include the API key (X-API-Key), Content-Type of application/json, and a User-Agent header. Cookie data is only included in the request when explicitly provided via the cookies option, and filter rules are applied to cookie values before transmission.

trigger_keywords: how does server delivery signal errors to the callback
what does the callback receive on HTTP failure
how are non-201 responses handled in server delivery
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On a successful delivery (HTTP 201), the callback receives the notice message and the report ID extracted from the JSON response body. On any non-success HTTP status, the callback receives the notice message, a null/none ID, an error type of 'http_status', and the actual numeric HTTP status code. The callback is always invoked — there is no silent failure path.

trigger_keywords: what does middleware clear on each request
when does the middleware clear per-request state
how does the request state middleware work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the request-state middleware is installed, it calls a clear() operation at the end of every request — both successful responses and error cases. The error-reporter middleware separately catches thrown route errors, calls notify() with the error, then forwards the error to the next error handler in the chain (it does not swallow errors). On routes that succeed without error, no notification is sent but clear() is still called once.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The installRequestState middleware in Feature 13 that calls Honeybadger.clear() after every request completes, resetting breadcrumbs and context so one request's data does not bleed into the next

context_pointers.vague_reference: cap out at a reasonable size so memory doesn't balloon
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The breadcrumb trail maximum of 40 records defined in Feature 8, where older entries are evicted when the queue is full and a new breadcrumb is added

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reportData override flag in ClientConfig (Feature 5) that bypasses environment-based delivery gating — mirrors a pattern previously used to force reporting in non-production environments for specific test or debug scenarios

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific detail off the top of my head — you'll want to dig into the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or grep the codebase for the relevant behavior. The test contracts are the ground truth for edge cases.

feature10_browser_global_error_events.js.description: Browser global error and unhandled rejection events are converted into normalized notices and error breadcrumbs while ignored cross-origin script errors are skipped.

cases.expected_output:
notify_count=1
name=window.onerror
message=expected message
stack=expected message
    at ? (https://www.example.com/:1:0)
breadcrumb_count=1
breadcrumb_message=window.onerror
breadcrumb_category=error

notify_count=0
name=none
message=none
stack=none
breadcrumb_count=0
breadcrumb_message=none
breadcrumb_category=none

notify_count=1
name=window.onunhandledrejection
message=UnhandledPromiseRejectionWarning: Honeybadgers!
stack=none
breadcrumb_count=0
breadcrumb_message=none
breadcrumb_category=none

feature13_http_middleware_integration.js.description: HTTP middleware clears per-request state, reports thrown route errors, forwards them to later error handlers, and leaves successful routes unreported.

feature1_filter_url_parameters.json.description: Sensitive URL query parameters named by configured filters are replaced while other URL text is preserved.

cases.expected_output: url=https://www.example.com/?secret=[FILTERED]

feature2_filter_structured_data.json.description: Object keys that partially match configured filter names are redacted recursively and case-insensitively.

cases.expected_output: data={"secret_key":"[FILTERED]"}

feature3_sanitize_payload_values.json.description: Payload data is sanitized by limiting nesting depth, dropping absent object fields, and replacing recursive or non-serializable values with neutral markers.

cases.expected_output: data={"one":{"two":{"three":"[DEPTH]"}}}

feature4_parse_stack_trace.json.description: A textual stack trace is converted into structured frame data with file, function, line, and column fields.

input.stack:
Error: Something unexpected has occurred.
    at bar (foo.js:1:2)

cases.expected_output: backtrace=[{"column":2,"file":"foo.js","method":"bar","number":1}]

feature5_notice_delivery_gating.json.description: Error notices are delivered only when reporting is configured and enabled for the current environment.

cases.expected_output: delivered=false

delivered=true
class=Error
message=test

feature6_notice_payload_mapping.json.description: A notice is transformed into a report payload that preserves supplied error identity, request data, context, details, and configurable metadata.

feature7_normalize_tags.json.description: Tags from notice data, context, and configuration are split on commas, stripped to word characters, ordered by precedence, and de-duplicated.

cases.expected_output: tags=["tag1","tag2","tag3","tag4","tag5"]
tags=["tag1","tag2","tag3","tag4"]

feature8_breadcrumb_trail.json.description: Breadcrumb records capture recent events with category and metadata, are included in notice payloads by default, and respect queue limits and disabling.
